coredata.dc:title: A simple and convenient electrochemiluminescence immunoassay by using gold nanoparticles as both label and co-reactant
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Changzhi
Niu, Lulu
Wang, Xiaoyu
Sun, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A simple and convenient electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECLIA) has been developed by using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as both label and co-reactant. In this novel ECLIA, the detection antibodies labeled with AuNPs as co-reactant reacts directly with ruthenium complex to produce electrochemiluminescence (ECL), which avoids the weakness of luminophore as a label. The feasibility of the new method was investigated through the sandwich ECL immunosensor for the determination of human immunoglobulin (HIgG), and excitation of luminescence was respectively driven by linear sweep voltammetry and step potential. Under the optimal conditions, ECL intensity of the immunosensor increased with HIgG concentration in a wide range from 0.01 to 10.0 ng/mL and displayed linear response to logarithm of HIgG concentration. The results showed that the detection limit was 5 pg/mL, and the relative error was no more than 4.6% for the repeated measurements. The relative standard deviation was less than 2.9% for the determination of the standard sample, which can meet the requirement of ECLIA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Immunoassay
Gold nanoparticles
Label
Co-reactants
Ruthenium complex

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CVs (A) and EISs (B) of the bare GCE (a), Ab-GCE (b), BSA/Ab-GCE (c), HIgG > Ab-GCE (d), AuNPs-Ab < HIgG > Ab-GCE (e) in 0.1 mol/L PBS containing 0.1 mol/L KCl and 3.0 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−.

LSVs (A) and ECL spectra (B) of 0.50 mmol/L Ru(bpy)3 2+ solution on bare GCE in the presence of AuNPs-labeled antibody (a and c), and on AuNPs-Ab < HIgG > Ab-GCE (b and d) at a sweep rate of 100 mV/s, respectively.

Illustration of optimization curves for the concentration of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (A), electrolyte pH (B), incubation temperature (C) and time (D) based on response of immunosensor to 1.0 ng/mL HIgG, respectively.

ECL signals (A) and ECL response curves (B) of immunosensor for detecting HIgG at concentrations of 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.09, 0.10, 0.30, 0.50, 0.70, 0.90, 1.00, 3.00, 5.00, 7.00, 9.00, 10.0 ng/mL (from left to right) in the presence of 0.10 mmol/L Ru(bpy)3 2+. Inset is the calibration plot on the ECL response to the logarithm of HIgG concentration within the concentration range of 0.03–10.0 ng/mL. Detection condition: 10 mL 0.1 M PBS and linear potential scanning from 0 to 1.50 V at 100 mV/s.

ECL signals (A) and ECL response curves (B) of immunosensor for HIgG detection at concentrations of 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.09, 0.10, 0.30, 0.50, 0.70, 0.90, 1.00, 3.00, 5.00, 7.00, 9.00, 10.0 ng/mL (from left to right) in the presence of 0.10 mmol/L Ru(bpy)3 2+. Inset is the calibration plot on the ECL response to the logarithm of HIgG concentration within the concentration range of 0.01–10.0 ng/mL. Detection condition: 10 mL 0.1 M PBS and continuous SP from 0 to 1.50 V at a duration time of 5 s.

ECL responses to 1.00 ng/mL HIgG at excitation by potential scanning (A) and continuous excitation by SP (B) at an interval of 15 s from 0 to 1.50 V in the presence of 0.1 mmol/L Ru(bpy)3 2+, respectively.
ECL immunoassay by using AuNPs as both label and co-reactant.
The result for determination of CRMs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations in Table 2: GN, Graphene nanosheet; FITC, Fluoresceiniso-thiocyanate; thiol, thiolacetic acid; DMSA, meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid; ABEI, N-(aminobutyl)-N-(ethylisoluminol); GLD, Glutaraldehyde; APS, 3-aminopropyl- triethoxysilane; CNT-CHIT, Carbon nanotubes-Chitosan; CdSe NCs, CdSe Nanocrystals.

head.ce:title: A simple and convenient electrochemiluminescence immunoassay by using gold nanoparticles as both label and co-reactant

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, The Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science & Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, The Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science & Technology, Qingdao 266042, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and convenient electrochemiluminescence immunoassay has been developed by using gold nanoparticles as both marker and co-reactant.

A simple and convenient electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECLIA) has been developed by using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as both label and co-reactant. In this novel ECLIA, the detection antibodies labeled with AuNPs as co-reactant reacts directly with ruthenium complex to produce electrochemiluminescence (ECL), which avoids the weakness of luminophore as a label. The feasibility of the new method was investigated through the sandwich ECL immunosensor for the determination of human immunoglobulin (HIgG), and excitation of luminescence was respectively driven by linear sweep voltammetry and step potential. Under the optimal conditions, ECL intensity of the immunosensor increased with HIgG concentration in a wide range from 0.01 to 10.0 ng/mL and displayed linear response to logarithm of HIgG concentration. The results showed that the detection limit was 5 pg/mL, and the relative error was no more than 4.6% for the repeated measurements. The relative standard deviation was less than 2.9% for the determination of the standard sample, which can meet the requirement of ECLIA.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECLIA), which combines electrochemilumine- scence (ECL) and immunoassay, is one of the most attractive method in biochemical analysis. The fusion of ECL and immunological technology has given ECLIA some unique advantages, such as high sensitivity, low background, broad dynamic range, easy to use, and unsurpassed performance [1,2]. ECLIA is a specific luminescent reaction initiated by electrochemical reaction on the electrode functionalized by immunologic recognition mechanism. Besides ECL reagent, it is related to two crucial problems, the co-reactant causing ECL reaction and the label responding to immune recognition. As a typical ECLIA using ruthenium complex (Ru(bpy)3 2+) as luminophore, its co-reaction reagent is usually tripropylamine (TPrA), and there are also reports of the use of amino acids, ascorbic acid or quantum dots, etc [3–5], but they are not suitable for labeling. Few composite nanomaterials were used as labels and co-reactants at the same time [6], and the complex preparation process will limit their application. When the co-reactant cannot be directly used as label, the derivatization of luminophore must be done to label the detection antibody. Despite Ru(bpy)3 2+ is already a common commercial reagent, its derivatives need to be specially synthesized to convenient for marking. Among them, the representative compound is tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium NHS ester. However, there is disadvantage of low sensitivity in directly labeling antibodies with these reagents. In order to solve the problem of signal amplification, various approaches have been proposed by using various methods [7–11], though the process of immunoassay may become cumbersome. If using other luminophores as label to replace Ru(bpy)3 2+ derivatives, the immunoassay could become more complex and high cost [12]. Therefore, seeking out a material that acts as both label and co-reactant, and investigating to its performance is very meaningful in ECLIA.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) is the most stable precious metal nanoparticle. It has been widely applied in many fields because of its high electron density, dielectric property, low toxicity and very good adsorption characteristics [13]. Unique chemical properties of AuNPs also include easy to functionalize and immobilize on the surface of the media [14–16]. As a commonly used label, AuNPs has been widely applied in clinical diagnosis, environmental protection and food safety by means of the nanogold-labellingtechnology [16–18]. Although AuNPs have been applied in ECL system as an ECL reagent or functional material, and the relevant reports indicated that it has a good development potential in ECL analysis [19–22], its role was constrained because it was not used as a co-reactant in ECL reaction. On the other hand, the number of the reported ECLIA has obviously increased in recent years [1,23,24], but the preparation of the immune electrode is becoming more and more complex, which leads to difficulties in practical application. If AuNPs plays the role of both label and co-reaction reagent, ECLIA will become simple and convenient, and it is worth considering for commercial application. Our discovery, AuNPs as co-reactant can participate in ECL reaction of Ru(bpy)3 2+ and it-labeled antibodies has a good concentration response in ECL reaction [25,26], it brings opportunities to achieve this goal. Based on this, we are going to report a simple and convenient ECLIA by using gold nanoparticles as both label and co-reactant in the paper.

Tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) chloride hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3 2+) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Beijing, China). Its stock solution (1.0 × 10−3 mol/L) was prepared with 0.1 mol/L phosphate buffer (PBS) of pH 7.4 and stored in the light-protected bottle. The working solutions of Ru(bpy)3 2+ were freshly prepared from the stock solution with PBS containing 0.05 mol/L KNO3. The human immunoglobulin (HIgG) and the goat anti-HIgG (Ab) were obtained from Nanjing Sanchen Biotechnology Co., LTD (Nanjing, China). The anti-HIgG labeled with 10 nm gold nanoparticles (AuNPs-Ab) was obtained from Cytodiagnostics Inc. (Burlington, Canada). Their low concentration standard solutions were freshly prepared through diluting the standard solutions with PBS before the experiment. The certified reference material of HIgG were obtained from National Standard Material Center (GBW 09817, Beijing, China) and used as the certified reference materials (CRMs). All other chemicals were of analytical grade or higher quality. All solutions were prepared by using ultrapure water (>18 MΩ).

ECL measurements were performed with an MPI-B multifunctional ECL analyzer (Xi’an Remex Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd. (Xi’an, China). The standard three electrodes arrangement consisted of a functionalized glassy carbon disk electrode (GCE, 3.0 mm diameter), a Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a Pt counter electrode. The emission window of ECL analyzer was placed in front of the photomultiplier tube biased at −800 V. After the sample and electrodes were put in the electrolytic cell for 1 min, then ECL responses were recorded by scanning potential in a suitable range, or starting step potential (SP) from 0 to 1.50 V at a duration time of 5 s. ECL spectrum was obtained from scanning the functionalized electrodes in Ru(bpy)3 2+ solutions by coupled with a CHI 660B electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, Co., Ltd., China) and a F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan), and an electrolytic cell with a side light window [27] was used. All measurements were repeated at least five times and results were evaluated with relative standard deviation (RSD).

For the proposed method, the preparation of ECL immunoprobe and the operation of immunoassay are simple and convenient. GCE was employed as basic electrode for preparing immunosensors because of good conductivity, high chemical stability, wide polarization range, and easy chemical modification. Following the steps shown in Schematic 1 , the pretreatment of GCE was performed by dipping the polished GCE in 4 mg/mL 3,4-dihydroxyphenylpropionic acid solution for 30 min under stirring to generate carboxyl groups on the electrode surface. The carboxyl groups of GCE surface was activated by 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (DEC), and Ab as capture antibodies was immobilized on the activated GCE surface to form an immunoprobe in the presence of crosslinking agent N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS). The coated antibody's concentration and incubation time were 50 ng/mL and 3.5 h. After bovine serum albumin (BSA) was used to block the vacant sites on the surface of the modified electrode, an incubation procedure enables the immunoprobe to capture samples (HIgG) of various concentrations under optimal incubation time of 45 min. Then, a sandwich method was completed by combining the detection antibody with HIgG (Ag) after the modified electrode was incubated for 60 min in 100 ng/mL AuNPs-Ab solution. Finally, the functionalized electrode was placed in the electrolyte containing Ru(bpy)3 2+, and ECL signals was obtained by applying potential to the immune electrode. In addition, multiple washing is necessary after each incubation step to remove the physical adsorbate. The process of preparing immunoprobe and recognizing antigen was characterized by the peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− on different electrodes and the electron-transfer resistance (Ret) of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitor for preparation of ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is a frequently used method for characterizing the modification stepwise in fabricating the sandwiched ECL immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 1 A, five redox peaks of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− showed almost the same ΔEp of 100 mV and i pa/i pc of 1.10 on different electrodes. Although the reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− on various electrodes is a quasi-reversible process based on ΔEp and i pa/i pc, it does not affect the characterization on the modification process of the electrode. After capture antibodies were immobilized on GCE surface, anode and cathode peak currents of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− decreased synchronously from 85 and 83 μA to 78 and 70 μA (curve b), which was due to the reason that protein as nonconductor could obstruct the electron transfer towards electrode surface. Subsequently, the peak currents further decreased after BSA, HIgG and AuNPs-Ab were respectively immobilized on the modified electrode (curve c, d and e), which is consistent with the fact that the hydrophobic layer of the protein insulates the conductive support and hinders the interfacial electron transfer. Although AuNPs have good conductivity, the peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was not increased (curve e) in the final step, indicating that the AuNPs linked to antibodies cannot affect the inertia of biomacromolecules for the electron transfer.

As an effective method to monitor the change of electrode surface, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed as control tests of each fabrication steps by measuring the electron-transfer resistance (Ret). EIS curves for different modified electrode were exhibited in Fig. 1B. It can be seen from the figure, the bare GCE has a very small semicircle (curve a), the Ret value calculated from Nyquist plots is 275 Ω. After non-electroactive antibodies were successfully assembled on GCE, the Ret (curve b) increased to 370 Ω, which might be due to that Ab hindered the electron transfer. With the BSA and HIgG (curve c and d) were successively assembled onto the surface of the modified electrode, the Ret increased in succession with value of 448 Ω and 530 Ω, ascribing to that biomolecules could severely block the electron transfer tunnel. Finally, when the signal probe was attached onto the modified electrode, the Ret increased to 625 Ω (curve e) because the AuNPs-Ab reduced the effective area of electron transfer. The resistances increased continuously as accumulation of biomacromolecules layer by layer on electrode surface (curve b–e), indicating that these non-conducting bioactive materials have been the successfully modified on the electrode.

New role of AuNPs as a co-reactant has been reported in ECL reaction of Ru(bpy)3 2+, and AuNPs as a label exhibited the same behavior in its ECL reaction with Ru(bpy)3 2+ [25]. As displayed in Fig. 2 , the ECL spectrum of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in the presence of AuNPs-labeled antibody has a unique illuminating peak at 620 nm (curve c) when applying a positive potential sweep from 0.70 to 1.50 V (curve a), which is consistent with the typical ECL characteristic of Ru(bpy)3 2+. After the immunoprobe captured the sample and formed a sandwich immunosensor with AuNPs-labeled antibody, almost the same linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) (curve b) and ECL spectrum (curve d) were obtained at AuNPs- Ab < HIgG > Ab-GCE in the presence of Ru(bpy)3 2+, which reveals that AuNPs as label can also act as a co-reacting reagent for ECL reaction of Ru(bpy)3 2+. Based on the ECL reaction mechanism of AuNPs and Ru(bpy)3 2+ [26], the principle of the recommended ECLIA can be easily given by eq. (1)–(5).
(1)
Ab + Ag
→ Ag > Ab
(2)
AuNPs - Ab\; + \;Ag
> Ab →
AuNPs - Ab
Ag
Ab
(3)
Ru
bpy
3
2 +
-
e -
→ Ru
bpy
3
3 +
(4)
Ru
bpy
3
3 +
+
\;AuNPs - Ab
Ag
Ab → Ru
bpy
3
2 + ∗
+
AuNPs(III) - Ab
Ag
Ab
(5)
Ru
bpy
3
2 + ∗
→ Ru
bpy
3
2 +
+ h v
62 0 \;nm

From the ECL reaction principle, we can see that the new method is a different idea from the typical ECLIA. Here, the label, AuNPs act as a co-reactant simultaneously. The new role extends the application of AuNPs in ECLIA, not just as part of carriers or composite materials.

There are many parameters affecting the immunoassay, among which Ru(bpy)3 2+ concentration, electrolyte pH, incubation temperature and time are important. Through orthogonal test, the best experimental conditions were obtained and summarized in Fig. 3 . As ECL reagent, the concentration of Ru(bpy)3 2+ significantly affects the intensity of signal in the ECL system of Ru(bpy)3 2+/AuNPs. As shown in Fig. 3A, the responses of ECL immunosensor to 0.10 ng/mL HIgG stepped up gradually with the increase of the Ru(bpy)3 2+ concentration from 0.03 to 0.10 mmol/L, which was attributed to the increasing luminophore generated on the surface of electrode. When the Ru(bpy)3 2+ concentration exceeds 0.10 mmol/L, ECL intensities of immune response is no longer increased, which indicates that the ECL reaction has reached a dynamic balance. Thus, 0.10 mmol/L Ru(bpy)3 2+ was selected as the optimum concentration. The effect of pH (5.0–8.5) on the ECL response of the immunosensor was shown in Fig. 3B. Obviously, the immunosensor showed the best performance at pH 7.5. The reason may be that the environment peracid or peralkaline is not conducive to the biological activity of proteins and the immune reaction. Based on this result, 7.5 was employed as pH of the electrolyte. In addition, the effect of incubation temperature and time on the response of the sensing system was also evaluated. In Fig. 3C, ECL responses of the immunoprobe to the sample increased gradually and reached a plateau at about 35 °C. And ECL responses took place small change when the time more than 45 min, indicating that the immunoreaction has been completed on the electrode surface (Fig. 3D). According to the experimental results, 35 °C and 45 min were determined as the optimal incubation temperature and time in the experiment.

In order to investigate the feasibility of the proposed method, determination of HIgG was respectively performed by using two electrochemical technique for initiating luminescence, which are linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and step potential (SP). LSV is atypical voltammetrywhere the potential applied on the working electrode as linear function of time, and the range of potential scanning can be controlled in a certain range. It can be used to observe what reactions may occur in the whole range of potential and results caused by electrode reactions. SP method is a technique to obtain current response quickly. For this method, the potential of the working electrode is controlled to change according to a predetermined rule, and the change of electrode current with time is measured at the same time, and then the substance causing the current change is quantified [28]. SP technique was selected as another method to obtain ECL immune responses because of its advantage of less reagent consumption, good repeatability and saving time. Under the optimum experimental conditions, ECL signals and the response curve of the immunosensor to HIgG at various concentrations obtained by using LSV method were showed in Fig. 4 . As Fig. 4 indicates, ECL intensities increased steadily with the increase of HIgG concentrations from 0.01 to 10.0 ng/mL (Fig. 4A) and displayed a logarithmic trend. Within the concentration range of 0.03–10.0 ng/mL (Fig. 4B), ECL intensities were proportional to logarithm of HIgG concentrations (the insertion in Fig. 4B, R2 = 0.9986), and detection limit was less than 10 pg/mL.

ECL signals of the immunosensor to HIgG at different concentrations were obtained by SP driving (Fig. 5 A), and the response curve was showed in Fig. 5B. As can be seen from Fig. 5, the ECL response of the immunosensor to samples increased steadily with the increase of HIgG concentrations (Fig. 5A) and displayed a logarithmic pattern (Fig. 5B). In the concentration range of 0.01–10.0 ng/mL, ECL intensities were proportional to logarithm of HIgG concentrations (the insertion in Fig. 5B, R2 = 0.9965) with a detection limit of 5 pg/mL. From the results of the investigation, SP-driven determining range and sensitivity were better than that of LSV method, and the time required for multiple measurements was also less.

It is a regular practice by using the Certified Reference Materials (CRMs) to determine the accuracy of a new analytical method. According to the proposed method, the CRMs containing 11.1 ng/mL HIgG were determined by two driving techniques respectively (Table 1 ). The measured value was HIgG concentration in the original sample without addition of standard, while the found value was the total concentrations of HIgG with addition of standard. The measurement results showed that sample recoveries were between 95% and 106% and with RSD within 2.5–2.9%, giving an acceptable accuracy and precision.

It is known that antibodies can be attached to AuNPs through physisorption or chemisorption to form stable AuNPs-Ab complex. When Ru(bpy)3 3+ react with AuNPs on AuNPs-Ab < HIgG > Ab-GCE (eq. (4)), the resulted Au(III) may improve the stability of the complex due to the increase of ionic potential of AuNPs, which is conducive to the repeatability of the method. But the change of electrostatic property of AuNPs surface will have a negative effect on the adsorption of Ru(bpy)3 2+. In the face of this complex problem, it is a good scheme to investigate its effect on repeatability through experiments. The repeated responses of immunosensor were showed in Fig. 6 , ECL intensities displayed repeatable and stable responses to HIgG using LSV drive (Fig. 6A) or SP intermittent excitation (Fig. 6B). The relative error of eight repeated responses was 4.6% to the former, and the relative error of nine repeated responses was 3.8% to the latter, which show a better repeatability for the recommendedapproach. The reproducibility and storage stability are the major concerns for practical application of immunosensors. The reproducibility of the prepared immunosensor was investigated by prepared seven parallel electrodes under the same conditions, and RSD was 6.7% for 1.00 ng/mL HIgG. When the immunosensor was stored at 4 °C for 20 days, the ECL response decreased by 5.8% for detecting 1.00 ng/mL HIgG. After the immunosensor was stored 30 days in the same conditions, the ECL signal was reduced to 91% of the original.

There have been many reports for determination of HIgG by using ECLIA [29–34]. Comparing with these reports (Table 2 ), the composition of immunosensor and the ECL system used in the presented method is the simplest, although its detection range and limit is not the best. By considering the convenience of application and the simplicity for preparation of immunosensor, the presented method provides a new commercial platform for ECLIA.

In summary, we have introduced a simple and convenient ECLIA using AuNPs instead of luminophore as label and co-reactant at the same time. The performance of the new method was verified by the determination of HIgG with sandwich-type immunosensor. Benefiting from its unique characteristics, AuNPs exhibited simple and convenient advantage as both label and co-reactant in the preparation of immunosensors and actual measurements. The immunosensor prepared by the proposed method showed good accuracy, excellent repeatability, reproducibility, and long-term stability in HIgG analysis. More importantly, this new strategy is low in cost and convenient operation due to combination ofECL and nanogold-labelling technology, and it will be more suitable for clinical application as ECLIA by combining with detection equipment in the future. We believe that this research provides a reliable approach and helpful inspiration for further development of Ru(bpy)3 2+-based ECL system.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully appreciate the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of Hainan Province (2017CXTD007), the Key Science Technology Program of Haikou City (2017042), and the Open-end Fund of Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, the Ministry of Education (OESACLS201901).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Li, Y. Chen, J.-J. Zhu, Recent advances in electrochemiluminescence analysis, Anal. Chem. 89 (2017) 358−371.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of electrogenerated chemilumine- scent biosensors for assays in biological matrices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. M. Gross, S. S. Maddipati, S. M. Snyder, A review of electrogenerated chemilumine- scent biosensors for assays in biological matrices, Bioanalysis 8 (2016) 2071–2089.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence immunosensor based on L-Lys-Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ functionalized porous six arrises column nanorods for detection of CA15-3

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, Y. He, H. Wang, Y. Yuan, R. Yuan, Y. Chai, A self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence immunosensor based on L-Lys-Ru(dcbpy)32+ functionalized porous six arrises column nanorods for detection of CA15-3, Biosens. Bioelectron. 74 (2015) 924−930.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescence of tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(ii) with ascorbic acid and dehydroascorbic acid in aqueous and non-aqueous solutions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Takahashi, K. Hattori, M. Matsuoka, J. Jin, Electrochemiluminescence of tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(ii) with ascorbic acid and dehydroascorbic acid in aqueous and non-aqueous solutions, Anal. Sci. 32 (2016) 443−447.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of pentachlorophenol by anodic electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+ based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as co-reactant reactant

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Luo, L. Li, X. Xu, D. Liu, J. Li, K. Wang, T. You, Determination of pentachlorophenol by anodic electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)32+ based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as co-reactant reactant, RSC Adv. 7 (2017) 50634–50642.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-potential electrochemiluminescence ratiometric sensor for sensitive detection of dopamine based on graphene-CdTe quantum dots and selfenhanced Ru(II) complex

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Fu, X. Tan, R. Yuan, S. Chen, A dual-potential electrochemiluminescence ratiometric sensor for sensitive detection of dopamine based on graphene-CdTe quantum dots and selfenhanced Ru(II) complex, Biosens. Bioelectron. 90 (2017) 61–68.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Variable doping induces mechanism swapping in electrogenerated chemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+ core−shell silica nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Valenti, E. Rampazzo, S. Bonacchi, L. Petrizza, M. Marcaccio, M. Montalti, L. Prodi, F. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrogenerated chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer between Ru(bpy)3 2+ electrogenerated chemiluminescence and gold nanoparticles/graphene oxide nanocomposites with graphene oxide as coreactant and its sensing application
